body:
### Describe the feature

It seems the "clk" syntax is working fine, but some websites also use the "timestamp" syntax (chess.com)

When you export a game on chess.com it adds both time signatures as comments. Only one of them is processed by en croissant, and the second signature clutters the comments. If the game happens to have other comments as well, it can make a mess of things.

`1. e4 {[%clk 0:09:59.9][%timestamp 1]} 1... e5 {[%clk 0:09:59.2][%timestamp 8]}
2. Bc4 {[%clk 0:09:58.8][%timestamp 11]} 2... ;etc`

A partial implementations would be to recognize the string and filter it out. Still using the same timestamp as before.
A better solution is to support both, but only use the 2nd style as a fallback the first.

As a side note loading chess.com games via the URL method, doesn't seem to load any timestamps.
title: Support PGN Timestamp using the "[%timestamp 123]" syntax

### Describe the feature

Hi, I am a big fan of this software, works very well, has a sleek UI, isn’t too buggy, etc. Much nicer UI than most other chess software. However, my one gripe is that you can’t generate move anotations without generating a full game report. You have to sit there and wait for the report to finish, which can take a while if you want to analyze at a high depth. My understanding is that the annotations are just based on the engine’s analysis of the position. So, why can’t there be a feature where you get move annotations as you analyze the game yourself, alongside the engine evaluation? I think chess.com analysis has something similar to this, where in their analysis, when you start exploring variations, or just analyzing outside of a game review, you will get move annotations alongside the engine evaluation. To me, this is the one big feature missing from this software, and I would greatly appreciate any insight into if this is something feasible, in development, not planned, etc. Thanks for the great software!
title: Feature Request: Move annotations from the engine generated per move in analysis

body:
**Description**
- In practice mode, repeatedly using the left/right arrow keys sometimes lands on neighboring main lines instead of resuming the problem you were working on. Once you move left past the current drill, pressing right should always return you to the same practice line.
- When “See Answer” hasn’t been pressed yet, navigation should be locked to the current problem position. Today the move is blocked visually, but capture/move sounds still play, giving the impression that a move happened. In some cases you can even advance into future moves without revealing the answer.

**Steps to Reproduce**
1. Enter practice mode with at least one saved card.
2. Use the left arrow several times to move to the start, then press right—occasionally you’ll jump into an unintended variation.
3. Start a new practice card, keep the answer hidden, and press the right arrow. The move/capture audio still plays, and sometimes the position advances when it shouldn’t.

**Expected Behavior**
- Arrow navigation in practice mode always tracks the selected drill line; returning with the right arrow should land back on that line.
- While the answer remains hidden, navigation beyond the problem node is blocked silently—no move or sound should occur until the user presses “See Answer.”

**Proposed Fix**
I make the PR #632 to fix this issue. Add practice-specific navigation state (current practice path, whether practice mode is active, and whether the answer has been revealed). Use it to:
- Force `goToNext` to follow the stored practice path.
- Prevent advancing past the practice node until `See Answer` is triggered.
- Only play move sounds after the guard allows the move.
title: [Fix] Practice mode arrows can leave the drill line and play sounds before revealing the answer

### Describe the bug

Starting with v0.12.0 and continuing in v0.12.1, the piece-move animations during Analysis mode have become noticeably stuttery and sluggish. This performance regression does not occur in v0.11.1, where animations are perfectly smooth under identical settings.

The issue appears specifically when running multi-line analysis and seems tied to the migration to Tauri 2, because I observe the exact same behavior in pawn-appetite, which also recently migrated to Tauri 2.

### Reproduction

1. Open En Croissant v0.12.0 or v0.12.1
2. Enable Analysis mode with:
    - Lines: 3 lines
    - Threads/Cores: 4 cores
    - Hash size: 8 GB
3. move pieces quickly and observe piece movement animations as engine lines update

Expected Behavior
- Animation should remain smooth and fluid, as it was in v0.11.1, regardless of engine activity.

Actual Behavior
- Piece movement animations stutter, feel laggy, and are not smooth.
- Performance is noticeably degraded compared to v0.11.1.

### Platform and versions

Environment

- CPU: Intel Core i9-13900 (24 cores / 32 threads)
- OS: Windows 11
- En Croissant Versions Tested:
   - v0.11.1 — smooth animation
   - v0.12.0 — stutter present
   - v0.12.1 — stutter present

Stockfish 17.1 Engine Settings:
- 3 lines
- 4 cores
- 8 GB hash

Additional Notes
- The regression began immediately after the Tauri 2 migration, so it may be related to changes in rendering or event-loop performance.
- The same issue appears in pawn-epitite, another project that migrated to Tauri 2, which supports this theory.

Request
- Please investigate this issue, as smooth piece-move animation was one of the highlights of v0.11.1. The stuttering feels like a significant downgrade in user experience.
- If you need logs, profiling data, or a screen recording, I can provide them.

### Stack trace

```Shell

```
title: Piece-move animation stutters during Analysis mode after upgrade to Tauri 2 (v0.12.0+)

body: ### Describe the feature Hey! First of all, thank you a lot for a wonderful application (I hope you enjoy your croissants with coffee 😉)! I started using it to complement my studies on Lichess, but now I'm looking into completely replacing it. It might be just that I'm not used to UI, but I find few things very inconvenient when building a repertoire. Here's the scenario: - I create a repertoire file, i.e. "Scotch Game" - I used my chess.com game to study a line. - (A) I found the right answer that I want to expand on and made it the main line - (B) The actual game is now a sideline where I want to keep adding comments to compare the position that I can get with the right answer. - Ideally I want to keep adding more games as side lines as I go. Now the first problem (1) is that the game is named "kossnocor - GarrettBeck18" without a clear way to change it. I know that I can probably edit PNG myself, but it would be nice to have this option in the UI. Forgive me if I missed it! Another issue (2) is that the game is still says "Black wins" even though I changed the main line: I would expect the note to disappear or ideally move into the sideline block. Maybe that's a PNG limitation, but I would not mind if the result would transform into a comment on the last sideline move. I know that it might be a big ask, but having the ability to edit comments inline, right in this view would be amazing (3). When I analyze with an engine, it would be awesome to preserve the engine analysis until I restart it (4), so I can refer to past results when switching between moves. Now when I run the engine for deep analysis and then try another move, when I get back, it starts from scratch. I'm not sure if engines allow it, but having a dump so I can continue where I ended would be just perfect. I know that most of the requests might be a lot of work and I willing to try my hand on it and contribute to the app or help otherwise. If you like any of the ideas and don't mind me sending your a PR, or if have further questions, please let me know. I might have further ideas on how to improve UX, especially when using the app to build a repertoire. Please tell me if you would like to receive them as separate issues without an expectation that you would address any of them, just for the discussion's sake. Cheers!
title: UX improvements when building a repertoire

body:
### Describe the feature

Description

Allow users to select a move or a position and request an AI-powered explanation in natural language, optionally read aloud using a voice of their choice (e.g. male/female, tone, language).

This would make En-Croissant feel like a personal coach, not just an analysis tool.

⸻

🎯 Use Cases
	•	📚 Study Mode: Understand why a move is good or bad, especially for beginners or intermediate players.
	•	🎤 Voice Feedback: Make study more engaging and accessible — e.g., while multitasking or for visually impaired users.
	•	🧑‍🏫 Coaching: Students can study with narrated explanations for annotated games.

⸻

🛠️ Implementation Plan (Conceptual)

1. AI Explanation Logic

Use a GPT-based model or a local prompt-based logic that:
	•	Takes FEN + best move or sequence.
	•	Returns a paragraph like:
“Black plays …e5 here to contest the center and open up lines for the dark-squared bishop. It’s a classical response in the Italian Game.”

Options:
	•	Use an online API (e.g., OpenAI API, local llama.cpp inference).
	•	Or: build a rule-based explanation engine with templates based on evaluation, piece activity, threats, etc.

2. Voice Narration

Use text-to-speech libraries or services:
	•	Web TTS APIs (if browser-based)
	•	OS-native APIs (macOS/Windows have this)
	•	Integrate with open-source voices like [eSpeak](http://espeak.sourceforge.net/) or [Coqui TTS](https://coqui.ai/)

3. UI
	•	Right-click move → “Explain with AI”
	•	Toggle: “🔊 Read explanation aloud”
	•	Voice selector in settings (female/male, accent, etc.)
title: AI Move Explanation (with Optional Voice Narration)

### Describe the feature

The ability to save lines evaluated by the engine to the variations without jumping to that position to remove the reevaluation of the same line.

For example, after a e4, e5 opening the engine (stockfish 17 at 30 depth) recommends,
        2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bb5 Nf6 4. O-O Nxe4 5. Re1 Nd6 6. Nxe5 Be7 7. Bf1 Nxe5 8. Rxe5 O-O 9. d4 Bf6 10. Re1 Nf5 11. c3 
        d5 12. Nd2 Nd6 13. Nb3 Re8 14. Rxe8+ Nxe8 15. Nc5 c6 16. Qf3 g6 17. Bf4 Be7 18. a4 

as the best line to play and for the second-best line it recommends,
        2. Nc3  Nc6 3. Bc4 Nf6 4. d3 Na5 5. Nge2 Bc5 6. O-O O-O 7. Ng3 Nxc4 8. dxc4 d6 9. Qf3 h6 10. h3 Be6 11. Nd5 
        Nxd5 12. cxd5 Bd7 13. Be3 Bxe3 14. Qxe3 a5 

but you can't get it into this form,
        1. e4 {[%eval +0.21] } e5 {[%eval +0.21] } 2. Nf3  (2. Nc3  Nc6 3. Bc4 Nf6 4. d3 Na5 5. Nge2 Bc5 6. O-O O-O 7. 
        Ng3 Nxc4 8. dxc4 d6 9. Qf3 h6 10. h3 Be6 11. Nd5 Nxd5 12. cxd5 Bd7 13. Be3 Bxe3 14. Qxe3 a5 {[%eval -0.01] }) 
        Nc6 3. Bb5 Nf6 4. O-O Nxe4 5. Re1 Nd6 6. Nxe5 Be7 7. Bf1 Nxe5 8. Rxe5 O-O 9. d4 Bf6 10. Re1 Nf5 11. c3 d5 12. 
        Nd2 Nd6 13. Nb3 Re8 14. Rxe8+ Nxe8 15. Nc5 c6 16. Qf3 g6 17. Bf4 Be7 18. a4 {[%eval +0.32] } *

without going back to e5 creating unnecessary evaluation which in more complicated positions or higher depths could take a long time to do. I would recommend a button that toggles between the mode where it skips to that position and then one where it just marks the position in the lines / variations. Another way it could be implemented is a by having a way to copy the line to the clipboard. This would allow an easier export of evaluation lines to note sheets and other documents.
title: Save Lines from Evaluations

### Describe the bug

- When computer runs out of time it crashes
- If computer gets 1ms to think but it starts at 0.9ms at the end of ms, it plays illegal move and freezes
- If computer plays quickly like depth 2 vs depth 3 or ms then it also freezes even with legal move
- Computer wastes time loading its engine while clock is counting down -> **Solution:**Let engine load before timer starts
- Engine takes 30s to load on a slow computer then crashes if taking too long -> **Solution:**When you reach engine tab it can show all the stats of the engine since it doesn't need to be loaded
- Large databases take a long time to load on slow computer then crashes if it takes too long -> **Solution:**Just load the names and stats of the database then load it while the user is on the page or when the user clicks on the database and wants to see games , people, tournaments, etc
- Syzygypath not saving location sometimes
- Switching file location of en croissant just makes another file and doesn't delete the old one and also sometimes doesn't switch
- Downloading engines directly from en croissant (i tested leela in this case) doesn't download and crashes saying failed to fetch files -> might be a download issue and might need to just change download location
- If there's no internet and you open tablebase it crashes -> **Solution:**It can replace the tablebase icon with "_There is currently no internet_" but even better if you have a syzygypath (using custom syzygy path setting for analysis or engines) then it can use it
- If engines take too long to load and you switch to home page it crashes but reload fixes it -> **Solution:**Just load engine stats from previous and it will be faster and no crashes, also the issue might be quitting process
- The X in the corner used for closing windows doesn't close it and instead a very small sliver of that x is how you leave application
- The download percentages for your personal games are sometimes off like 0.0% if you just get on and have a slow computer and 101% after loading the full thing sometimes (only the chess.com was 101% in my case)

body:
### Describe the feature

The Woodpecker method has been known to rapidly improve tactical pattern recognition and lead to a significant boost in chess ratings. It's basically solving a fixed number of chess tactics (100-200 for the amateur, 1000 for the serious professional), and then going back and solving the same puzzles again, this time aiming to do it faster than before. 

This feature would greatly benefit all the chess players who are looking to improve their game. This would also set en-croissant apart from all the other open-source tools as none of them have this feature, leading to greater adoption of en-croissant by chess players of all levels. 

At a minimum, implementation of this feature would require a way to create puzzle sets from a puzzle database (lichess for example), based on ratings and/or motifs (if the information is available in the puzzle database). 

A tracker to keep track of the days taken to finish a puzzle set would be nice to have but is not a core requirement.
Example for a tracker: 
Cycle 1: 500 puzzles | 40 hours | 87% accuracy
Cycle 2: 500 puzzles | 18 hours | 92% accuracy
Cycle 3: 500 puzzles | 8.75 hours | 95% accuracy
title: Woodpecker method (spaced repetition) for puzzle training

body: ### Describe the feature I appreciate all the work done on En Croissant. It is already the best alternative on the market for me (I think it's awesome). After using it for a while, I have a couple suggestions for improvements. First, regarding the inline notation: - [ ] Remove the moving of longer comments to new lines - [ ] Include full move number after comments - [ ] Better nesting of variations Currently comments are moved in their entirety to new lines if they exceed the available leftover line width. This makes the hierarchy harder to follow, it also creates a confusing inconsistency with shorter comments. Keeping comments true inline, regardless of length, would improve readability in my opinion (see comparison below). Another thing that could aid in readability is having the full move number displayed whenever a comment preceeds the move. Compare *2…Nc6* with *Nc6* below. I think overall it's a lot clearer to (a) have the move "true inline", and (b) with the full move number (compared to alone on it's own line with no move number). And for the third point, take a look at *3… exd4 4. e5* below. Is the nesting/grouping not really confusing compared to the way Lichess handles it? I've been struggling to navigate sub-variations a lot when analysing my games, and I think this is a big part of it. *Lichess* *En Croissant* A few other feature requests not related to the inline notation: - [ ] Add shortcut to open current board position in a new tab and analysis board directly It would be more convenient than the manual method of "opening the info tab, selecting the FEN, copying it, opening a new tab, choosing analysis board, pasting it". And lifting certain positions from games must be a fairly common user action I imagine, at least it is for me. Having a shortcut below the board (next to the present ones) for this would be great. - [ ] Ability to add comment by right-clicking in the move list directly, or below the board The vertical space for the move list is quite valuable, and allowing the full right side of the screen to be occupied by the move list while still being able to annotate would be a great feature. Also, since there is empty space below the board, an expandable glyph section there (combined with the right click feature to add comments directly) might be a good solution? Looking forward to seeing how the project evolves in the future!
title: Inline notation improvements (and other suggestions)

body:
### Overview
A self-contained user profile with a configurable location which allows for separation and organization of chess knowledge.

### Use Cases
User profiles enable such scenarios:
- A Coach using a separate profile for each of their students.
- A tournament player making opening preparations for a specific events.
- A shared profile for multiple users to use on a distributed system.
- Easy backups and versioning of the profiles themselves.

### Details
The application could have menu for switching user profiles in the bottom left corner, and an interface to back them up or manage them.

### Considerations
Implementing custom user profiles will require additional complexity in the Installer and codebase, including configuring the behavior of the application surrounding the profile.
title: [Proposal] User Profiles

body:
### Overview
A plugin API allowing for extensibility and fine-grained use cases for users and developers who want to integrate with or extend En croissant in some form.

### Use cases
The plugin API would some use cases like this:
- **Integration with other Applications.** - I'm a user of Obsidian and use it for Personal Knowledge Management, currently there are not enough good plugins to allow for a fully-fledged PKM system for Chess. En Croissant in a great candidate as a dedicated application which could then be integrated into Obsidian with a plugin, allowing for a comprehensive Chess PKM system which currently does not exist.
- **Extensibility.** - Alot of users often have specific, fine-grained use cases which cannot be met by the core functionality of an application alone, so a Plugin API would allow for solutions to be brought forward
- **Custom Positional Evaluations** - [In this blog on lichess.org](https://lichess.org/@/jk_182/blog/calculating-the-sharpness-of-different-players/JgqgiwXI), someone came up with a way to evaluate the position's "sharpness" by using Lc0, plugins could allow for such custom evaluations to be done.

There are no limits to what community plugin developers can do; these are just a few example cases of what's possible.

### Workarounds
None, except that custom engines and databases can be added currently.

### Considerations
- Plugin systems often introduce a large amount of complexity and can be very demanding to maintain effectively, affecting both the ecosystem and codebase. 
- Plugin APIs introduce security risks as they require loading third-party components, plugins need to be either reviewed or sandboxed to address the possibility of vulnerabilities being introduced.
title: [Proposal] Plugin API for Extensibility

body:
### Describe the feature

![image](https://github.com/franciscoBSalgueiro/en-croissant/assets/40061628/637eb641-152f-4786-8bc2-9e03e383f60c)
This is an example where Scid finds a duplicated game because on game is included in another game.
Ath the moment en-croissant don't detect these doubles.
Scid only detect these count of doubles when the player names are identical
I also want an option to detect these doubles when player names are different or other information like event is different. Than I want en-croissant to keep the better game, (longer game, more recent, higher elo, longer time control ,etc).
Maybe it are technically not exact doubles. But I don't see how it can be useful to keep games with exact the same moves, and exact the same results, together in a database.
Doubles where one game is included in another happens quite often in human tournaments when using DGT boards, sometimes the feed gets an extra move or a database feed like TWIC does an update one week and provides a game correction the next week.

I have made a Python script for myself to delete these duplicates, with the help of Microsoft Copilot Pro.
I'm not a programmer so it could be that there are mistakes in the script, (please let me know if you find any).

Here is the readme.txt of the script:
Remove_duplicates_and_prefixes.py This script removes duplicate games from a given input PGN file and writes the unique games to an output PGN file named duplicates_removed.pgn. Steps to use:
1.	Save the script in a directory on your computer.
2.	Open a terminal or command prompt.
3.	Navigate to the directory where you saved the script using the cd command.
4.	Run the script by typing python remove_duplicates_and_prefixes.py and press Enter. The script will look for an input file named input.pgn in the same directory.

Here is the script:

```
import chess.pgn
import os
import sys
from datetime import datetime

def get_quality_score(game):
    rating_weight = 0.4
    recency_weight = 0.2
    time_format_weight = 0.2
    length_weight = 0.2
    white_rating = int(game.headers.get('WhiteElo', '0'))
    black_rating = int(game.headers.get('BlackElo', '0'))
    average_rating = (white_rating + black_rating) / 2
    date_str = game.headers.get('Date', '1800.01.01')
    date = datetime.strptime(date_str, '%Y.%m.%d')
    recency = (datetime.now() - date).days
    event = game.headers.get('Event', '').lower()
    time_format_score = 1 if 'bullet' in event else 2 if 'blitz' in event else 3
    length = len(list(game.mainline_moves()))
    quality_score = (rating_weight * average_rating) - (recency_weight * recency) + (time_format_weight * time_format_score) + (length_weight * length)
    return quality_score

def remove_duplicates_and_prefixes(input_pgn_path, output_pgn_path):
    games = {}
    with open(input_pgn_path) as pgn:
        while True:
            game = chess.pgn.read_game(pgn)
            if game is None:
                break
            moves = str(game.mainline_moves())
            if moves not in games:
                games[moves] = game
            else:
                old_game = games[moves]
                new_game = game
                old_score = get_quality_score(old_game)
                new_score = get_quality_score(new_game)
                if new_score > old_score:
                    games[moves] = game

    with open(output_pgn_path, "w") as output_pgn:
        for game in games.values():
            output_pgn.write(str(game))
            output_pgn.write("\n\n")

def main():
    try:
        input_pgn_path = sys.argv[1]
        output_pgn_path = "duplicates_removed.pgn"
        remove_duplicates_and_prefixes(input_pgn_path, output_pgn_path)
    except FileNotFoundError as e:
        print(f"File not found: {e.filename}")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"An error occurred: {str(e)}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

```

    
    It would be nice if some similar functionality to detect these duplicates, could be added to en-croissant
title: Request option to detect doubles when games are included in other games.

## Issues
- [x] After changing a tab the report will not be shown
- [ ] Trying to generate a report with an engine that doesn't support multipv will crash
- [x] Unfinished reports
	`1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bb5 Nf6 4. O-O Nxe4 *`
	`1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bb5 Nf6 4. d3 Bc5 5. c3 O-O 6. O-O d6 *`
- [x] If a report starts with black to move and white makes the evaluation go up, it will trigger a mistake e.g. with a 100ms analysis with SF 16.
  ```
  [FEN "r4rk1/1pp3p1/p2p1qPn/3Pp3/2P1B1b1/5N2/PP2QPPR/1K1R4 b - - 4 26"]
  [SetUp "1"]

  26... Bf5 27.Nd2 Ng4 28.Qxg4 *
  ```
  ![image](https://github.com/franciscoBSalgueiro/en-croissant/assets/63931154/91aec060-33ab-4016-9ec7-38169e0d29d0)
- [x] The starting position is not being analyzed.
	This means that if the starting position has an evaluation very different from 0.15, the first move will be misevaluated. e.g. If white has piece odds, no matter what move it does it will be marked as a blunder or, if black has piece odds, if white makes a mistake it will not be marked as a blunder.

## Suggestions
- [x] Stop reports
- [ ] Live report of nags
- [x] Use the selected engine settings
- [x] More configurations (limits)
- [x] Do the analysis backwards
	With engines like Stockfish or Komodo this considerably increases the quality of the analysis, in addition to providing a more reliable and trustworthy one as well, which allows for the following:
	- [ ] Clean up the analysis
		When the analysis is finished, go through the moves forwards and:
		- If the player played the best move, we should use the same evaluation the previous position had. e.g if the best move in the starting position is 1. e4 and the evaluation in the starting position is +0.4, and after 1. e4 it was +0.3 then 1. e4 should increase to +0.4.
		- If its black to move and the evaluation is higher than in the previous position or, if its white to move and the evaluation is lower than in the previous position, we should use the same evaluation the previous position had.
	- [x] Add best, excellent, and good moves
		Base on these limits https://support.chess.com/article/2965-how-are-moves-classified-what-is-a-blunder-or-brilliant-and-etc
	- [x] Add great (!) moves
		(Change multipv to 2) If the played move was the best move, and if the played move was the only good move in the position, and it's not taking material, it should be "great".
	- [x] Add brilliant (!!) moves
		If the played move was the best move, and that move made a piece of higher value than the one we might or might not have taken be in danger, it should be "brilliant".
title: Game report
